**Chiswick** ([/ˈtʃɪzɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Chiswick.ogg "File:En-uk-Chiswick.ogg") [*CHIZ-ik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a district in [West London](/wiki/West_London "West London"), split between the [London Boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") of [Hounslow](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Hounslow "London Borough of Hounslow") and [Ealing](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Ealing "London Borough of Ealing"). It contains [Hogarth's House](/wiki/Hogarth%27s_House "Hogarth's House"), the former residence of the 18th-century English artist [William Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth"), [Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House"), a [neo-Palladian](/wiki/Neo-Palladian "Neo-Palladian") villa regarded as one of the finest in England and [Fuller's Brewery](/wiki/Fuller%27s_Brewery "Fuller's Brewery"), London's largest and oldest brewery. In a [meander](/wiki/Meander "Meander") of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") used for competitive and recreational rowing, with several rowing clubs on the river bank, the finishing post for [the Boat Race](/wiki/The_Boat_Race "The Boat Race") is just downstream of [Chiswick Bridge](/wiki/Chiswick_Bridge "Chiswick Bridge").

[Old Chiswick](/wiki/Old_Chiswick "Old Chiswick") was an [ancient parish](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Chiswick "St Nicholas Church, Chiswick") in the county of [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex"), with an agrarian and fishing economy beside the river; from the [Early Modern](/wiki/Early_Modern "Early Modern") period, the wealthy built imposing riverside houses on [Chiswick Mall](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall "Chiswick Mall"). Having good communications with London, Chiswick became a popular country retreat and part of the suburban growth of London in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It was made the [Municipal Borough of Brentford and Chiswick](/wiki/Municipal_Borough_of_Brentford_and_Chiswick "Municipal Borough of Brentford and Chiswick") in 1932 and part of [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") in 1965, when it merged into the [London Borough of Hounslow](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Hounslow "London Borough of Hounslow"). Modern Chiswick is an affluent area which includes the early garden suburb [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London"), [Grove Park](/wiki/Grove_Park,_Chiswick "Grove Park, Chiswick"), the Glebe Estate, [Strand-on-the-Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green") and [tube](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground") stations [Chiswick Park](/wiki/Chiswick_Park_tube_station "Chiswick Park tube station"), [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green_tube_station "Turnham Green tube station"), and [Stamford Brook](/wiki/Stamford_Brook_tube_station "Stamford Brook tube station"), as well as the [Gunnersbury Triangle](/wiki/Gunnersbury_Triangle "Gunnersbury Triangle") local nature reserve. Some parts of Bedford Park and [Acton Green](/wiki/Acton_Green,_London "Acton Green, London") are in the Chiswick W4 postcode area but the [London Borough of Ealing](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Ealing "London Borough of Ealing"). The main shopping and dining centre is [Chiswick High Road](/wiki/Chiswick_High_Road "Chiswick High Road").

[Chiswick Roundabout](/wiki/Chiswick_Roundabout "Chiswick Roundabout") is the start of the [North Circular Road](/wiki/A406_road "A406 road") (A406). At [Hogarth Roundabout](/wiki/Hogarth_Roundabout "Hogarth Roundabout"), the [Great West Road](/wiki/A4_road_(Great_Britain) "A4 road (Great Britain)") from central London becomes the [M4 motorway](/wiki/M4_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M4 motorway (Great Britain)"), while the [Great Chertsey Road](/wiki/A316_road "A316 road") (A316) runs south-west, becoming the [M3 motorway](/wiki/M3_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M3 motorway (Great Britain)").

People who have lived in Chiswick include the poets [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope") and [W. B. Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats"), the Italian poet and revolutionary [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo"), the painters [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh") and [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"), the novelist [E. M. Forster](/wiki/E._M._Forster "E. M. Forster"), the rock musicians [Pete Townshend](/wiki/Pete_Townshend "Pete Townshend"), [John Entwistle](/wiki/John_Entwistle "John Entwistle"), and [Phil Collins](/wiki/Phil_Collins "Phil Collins"), the stage director [Peter Brook](/wiki/Peter_Brook "Peter Brook"), and the actress [Imogen Poots](/wiki/Imogen_Poots "Imogen Poots").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/The_Old_Burlington_XVth_century_Church_Lane_Chiswick.JPG/250px-The_Old_Burlington_XVth_century_Church_Lane_Chiswick.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Old_Burlington_XVth_century_Church_Lane_Chiswick.JPG)

[Old Chiswick](/wiki/Old_Chiswick "Old Chiswick"): the fifteenth-century Old Burlington, one of two former pubs on Church Street, Chiswick. The tower of the former Lamb Brewery is behind it on the left.

Chiswick was first recorded c. 1000 as the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") *Ceswican* meaning 'Cheese Farm'; the riverside area of Duke's Meadows is thought to have supported an annual cheese fair up until the 18th century.[[3]](#cite_note-room-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The area was settled in Roman times; an urn found at Turnham Green contained Roman coins, and Roman brickwork was found under the [Sutton manor house](/wiki/Little_Sutton,_Chiswick "Little Sutton, Chiswick").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker1982Growth-5)

[Old Chiswick](/wiki/Old_Chiswick "Old Chiswick") grew up as a village around [St Nicholas Church](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Chiswick "St Nicholas Church, Chiswick") from c. 1181 on Church Street, its inhabitants practising farming, fishing and other riverside trades including a ferry, important as there were no bridges between London Bridge and Kingston throughout the Middle Ages.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199517-6) The area included three other small settlements, the fishing village of [Strand-on-the-Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green"), the hamlet of [Little Sutton](/wiki/Little_Sutton,_Chiswick "Little Sutton, Chiswick") in the centre, and [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green "Turnham Green") on the west road out of London.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199517-6)

A [decisive skirmish](/wiki/Battle_of_Turnham_Green "Battle of Turnham Green") took place on Turnham Green early in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). In November 1642, royalist forces under [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert "Prince Rupert"), marching from Oxford to retake London, were halted by a larger parliamentarian force under the [Earl of Essex](/wiki/Robert_Devereux,_3rd_Earl_of_Essex "Robert Devereux, 3rd Earl of Essex"). The royalists retreated and never again threatened the capital.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199529–30-7)

From 1758 until 1929 the [Dukes of Devonshire](/wiki/Duke_of_Devonshire "Duke of Devonshire") owned [Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House"), and their legacy can be found in street names all over Chiswick.[[a]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199538-8)

In 1864, [John Isaac Thornycroft](/wiki/John_Isaac_Thornycroft "John Isaac Thornycroft"), founder of the [John I. Thornycroft & Company](/wiki/John_I._Thornycroft_%26_Company "John I. Thornycroft & Company") shipbuilding company, established a yard at Church Wharf at the west end of [Chiswick Mall](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall "Chiswick Mall").[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) The shipyard built the first naval [destroyer](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"), [HMS *Daring*](/wiki/HMS_Daring_(1893) "HMS Daring (1893)") of the [Daring class](/wiki/Daring-class_destroyer_(1893) "Daring-class destroyer (1893)"), in 1893.[[11]](#cite_note-12) To cater for the increasing size of warships, Thornycroft moved its shipyard to [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton") in 1909.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199587–88-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Chiswick_High_Road_postcard.jpg/250px-Chiswick_High_Road_postcard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chiswick_High_Road_postcard.jpg)

Postcard photo of [Chiswick High Road](/wiki/Chiswick_High_Road "Chiswick High Road") and [King Street, Hammersmith](/wiki/King_Street,_Hammersmith "King Street, Hammersmith"), c. 1900

In 1822, the [Royal Horticultural Society](/wiki/Royal_Horticultural_Society "Royal Horticultural Society") leased 33 acres (13.4 ha) of land in the area south of the High Road between what are now Sutton Court Road and Duke's Avenue.[[13]](#cite_note-elliot-14) This site was used for its fruit tree collection and its first school of horticulture, and housed its first flower shows. The area was reduced to 10 acres (4.0 ha) in the 1870s, and the lease was terminated when the [Society's garden at Wisley](/wiki/RHS_Garden,_Wisley "RHS Garden, Wisley"), Surrey, was set up in 1904. Some of the original pear trees still grow in the gardens of houses built on the site.

The population of Chiswick grew almost tenfold during the 19th century, reaching 29,809 in 1901,[[14]](#cite_note-15) and the area is a mixture of Georgian, Victorian and Edwardian housing. Suburban building began in [Gunnersbury](/wiki/Gunnersbury "Gunnersbury") in the 1860s and in [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London"), the first [garden suburb](/wiki/Garden_suburb "Garden suburb"), on the borders of Chiswick and Acton, in 1875.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Chiswick was bombed repeatedly,[[16]](#cite_note-17) with both incendiary and high explosive bombs. Falling anti-aircraft shells and shrapnel also caused damage. The first [V-2 rocket](/wiki/V-2_rocket "V-2 rocket") to hit London fell on [Staveley Road](/wiki/Staveley_Road "Staveley Road"), Chiswick, at 6.43pm on 8 September 1944, killing three people, injuring 22 others and causing extensive damage to surrounding trees and buildings. Six houses were demolished by the rocket and many more suffered damage.[[17]](#cite_note-18) There is a memorial where the rocket fell on Staveley Road,[[18]](#cite_note-19) and a War Memorial at the east end of Turnham Green.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

[Refuge](/wiki/Refuge_(United_Kingdom_charity) "Refuge (United Kingdom charity)") was founded in 1971 in Chiswick, as the modern world's first safe house for women and children escaping domestic violence.[[20]](#cite_note-charity-commission.gov.uk-21)

By the start of the 21st century, Chiswick had become an affluent suburb.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Chiswick_Town_Hall_north_and_west_facing_515c.jpg/250px-Chiswick_Town_Hall_north_and_west_facing_515c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chiswick_Town_Hall_north_and_west_facing_515c.jpg)

[Chiswick Town Hall](/wiki/Chiswick_Town_Hall "Chiswick Town Hall"), designed by A. Ramsden, 1901[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Chiswick is split between the [London Boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") of [Hounslow](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Hounslow "London Borough of Hounslow") and [Ealing](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Ealing "London Borough of Ealing"). For elections to [Hounslow London Borough Council](/wiki/Hounslow_London_Borough_Council "Hounslow London Borough Council"), Chiswick is represented by nine councillors from three [electoral wards](/wiki/Electoral_wards "Electoral wards"): [Chiswick Gunnersbury](/wiki/Chiswick_Gunnersbury "Chiswick Gunnersbury"), [Chiswick Homefields](/wiki/Chiswick_Homefields "Chiswick Homefields") and [Chiswick Riverside](/wiki/Chiswick_Riverside "Chiswick Riverside").[[23]](#cite_note-24) Councillors serve four-year terms and since the [2022 Hounslow election](/wiki/2022_Hounslow_London_Borough_Council_election "2022 Hounslow London Borough Council election"), eight are [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") and one [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)").[[24]](#cite_note-25) Before 2022, the electoral wards were Turnham Green, Chiswick Homefields and Chiswick Riverside.[[25]](#cite_note-26) The Chiswick areas of [Acton Green](/wiki/Acton_Green,_London "Acton Green, London") and [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London") are part of [Ealing](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Ealing "London Borough of Ealing").

Chiswick forms part of the [Brentford and Isleworth](/wiki/Brentford_and_Isleworth "Brentford and Isleworth") parliamentary constituency, having been part of the [Brentford and Chiswick](/wiki/Brentford_and_Chiswick_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brentford and Chiswick (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency between 1918 and 1974.[[26]](#cite_note-1974constchanges-27) The [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) is [Ruth Cadbury](/wiki/Ruth_Cadbury "Ruth Cadbury") (Labour), elected at the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election") replacing [Mary Macleod](/wiki/Mary_Macleod "Mary Macleod") (Conservative).[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

For elections to the [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly"), Chiswick is in the [South West constituency](/wiki/South_West_(London_Assembly_constituency) "South West (London Assembly constituency)"), represented since 2024 by [Gareth Roberts](/wiki/Gareth_Roberts_(politician) "Gareth Roberts (politician)"), of the Liberal Democrats.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) It was one of 35 major centres identified in the statutory planning document of Greater London, the [London Plan](/wiki/London_Plan "London Plan") of 2008.[[32]](#cite_note-london_plan_f08-33)

Historically, Chiswick St Nicholas was an ancient parish in the [Ossulstone](/wiki/Ossulstone "Ossulstone") hundred of [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex").[[33]](#cite_note-vision_parish_chiswick-34) Until 1834 its [vestry](/wiki/Vestry "Vestry") governed most parish affairs. After the [Poor Law Amendment Act (1834)](/wiki/Poor_Law_Amendment_Act_1834 "Poor Law Amendment Act 1834"), local administration in Chiswick began to be devolved to authorities beyond the vestry. Then, Chiswick poor relief was administered by the Brentford [Poor Law Union](/wiki/Poor_law_union "Poor law union").[[34]](#cite_note-35) Briefly, from 1849 to 1855, responsibility for Chiswick drains and sewers passed to the [Metropolitan Commission of Sewers](/wiki/Metropolitan_Commission_of_Sewers "Metropolitan Commission of Sewers") under its 'Fulham and Hammersmith Sewer District.'[[35]](#cite_note-Logan-36) From 1858, under the Chiswick Improvement Act of that year,[[35]](#cite_note-Logan-36) responsibility for drains and sewers, paving and lighting was vested in an elected board of eighteen [Improvement Commissioners](/wiki/Improvement_commissioners "Improvement commissioners").[[35]](#cite_note-Logan-36) This operated as Chiswick's secular local authority for a quarter of a century until its replacement with a [Local Board](/wiki/Local_boards_formed_in_England_and_Wales_1848%E2%80%9394 "Local boards formed in England and Wales 1848–94") in 1883.[[35]](#cite_note-Logan-36) In 1878 the parish gained a triangle of land in the east which had formed a detached part of [Ealing](/wiki/Ealing "Ealing").[[36]](#cite_note-growth-37) From 1894 to 1927 the parish formed the [Chiswick Urban District](/wiki/Chiswick_Urban_District "Chiswick Urban District").[[37]](#cite_note-vision_chiswick-38)[[38]](#cite_note-EB1911-39) In 1927 it was abolished and its former area was merged with that of [Brentford Urban District](/wiki/Brentford_Urban_District "Brentford Urban District") to form [Brentford and Chiswick Urban District](/wiki/Municipal_Borough_of_Brentford_and_Chiswick "Municipal Borough of Brentford and Chiswick").[[39]](#cite_note-vision_brentford_chiswick-40) The amalgamated district became a municipal borough in 1932. The borough of Brentford and Chiswick was abolished in 1965, and its former area was transferred to [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") to form part of the London Borough of Hounslow.[[40]](#cite_note-lbhcreation-41) With these changes, [Chiswick Town Hall](/wiki/Chiswick_Town_Hall "Chiswick Town Hall") is no longer the local government centre but remains an approved venue for marriage and civil partnership ceremonies.[[41]](#cite_note-townhall-42)

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Corney_House_in_Chiswick_from_the_River_by_Jacob_Knyff_1675-80.jpg/500px-Corney_House_in_Chiswick_from_the_River_by_Jacob_Knyff_1675-80.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corney_House_in_Chiswick_from_the_River_by_Jacob_Knyff_1675-80.jpg)

Painting *Corney House in Chiswick from the River* by [Jacob Knyff](/wiki/Jacob_Knyff "Jacob Knyff"), 1675–80. [St Nicholas Church](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Chiswick "St Nicholas Church, Chiswick") is in the centre.

Chiswick occupies a meander of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"), 6 miles (9.7 km) west of [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross"). The district is built up towards the north with more open space in the south, including the grounds of [Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House") and [Duke's Meadows](/wiki/Duke%27s_Meadows "Duke's Meadows"). Chiswick has one main shopping area, the [Chiswick High Road](/wiki/Chiswick_High_Road "Chiswick High Road"), forming a long [high street](/wiki/High_street "High street") in the north, with additional shops on Turnham Green Terrace and Devonshire Road. The river forms the southern boundary with [Kew](/wiki/Kew "Kew"), including North Sheen, [Mortlake](/wiki/Mortlake "Mortlake") and [Barnes](/wiki/Barnes,_London "Barnes, London") in the [London Borough of Richmond upon Thames](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Richmond_upon_Thames "London Borough of Richmond upon Thames"). It includes the uninhabited island of [Chiswick Eyot](/wiki/Chiswick_Eyot "Chiswick Eyot"), joined to the mainland at low tide. In the east Goldhawk Road and British Grove border [Hammersmith](/wiki/Hammersmith "Hammersmith") in the [London Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Hammersmith_and_Fulham "London Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham"). In the north are [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London") (like Chiswick, within the London W4 postcode area) and [South Acton](/wiki/South_Acton,_London "South Acton, London") in the [London Borough of Ealing](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Ealing "London Borough of Ealing"), with a boundary partially delineated by the [District line](/wiki/District_line "District line"). On the west, within Hounslow, are the districts of [Gunnersbury](/wiki/Gunnersbury "Gunnersbury"), which is within the bounds of the early 19th century parish of Chiswick,[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199563-43) and [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford "Brentford").[[43]](#cite_note-map-44) A short distance south of the High Road in the centre of Chiswick is the Glebe Estate, consisting of small terraced houses built in the 1870s on [glebe](/wiki/Glebe "Glebe") land once owned by the local church, and now a desirable place to live.[[44]](#cite_note-45)
Chiswick is in the [W4 postcode district](/wiki/W_postcode_area "W postcode area") of the [London post town](/wiki/London_postal_district "London postal district"), which in a tribute to its ancient parish includes Bedford Park and [Acton Green](/wiki/Acton_Green,_London "Acton Green, London"), mostly within the London Borough of Ealing.[[45]](#cite_note-project-46)

Some of the most beautiful period mansion blocks in Chiswick, such as Heathfield Court and Arlington Mansions, line the sides of [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green "Turnham Green") – the site of the [Battle of Turnham Green](/wiki/Battle_of_Turnham_Green "Battle of Turnham Green") in 1642. Other suburbs of Chiswick include [Grove Park](/wiki/Grove_Park,_Chiswick "Grove Park, Chiswick") (south of the A4, close to Chiswick railway station) and [Strand on the Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green"), a fishing hamlet until the late 18th century.[[46]](#cite_note-47) As early as 1896, Bedford Park was advertised as being in Chiswick,[[47]](#cite_note-48) though at that time much of it was in [Acton](/wiki/Municipal_Borough_of_Acton "Municipal Borough of Acton").[[36]](#cite_note-growth-37)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Fuller%27s_brewery_-_pipes_-_cylinder.jpg/250px-Fuller%27s_brewery_-_pipes_-_cylinder.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fuller%27s_brewery_-_pipes_-_cylinder.jpg)

[Griffin Brewery](/wiki/Griffin_Brewery "Griffin Brewery"), [Old Chiswick](/wiki/Old_Chiswick "Old Chiswick")

Further information: [Chiswick High Road](/wiki/Chiswick_High_Road "Chiswick High Road")

Chiswick High Road contains a mix of retail shops, restaurants, food outlets and office and hotel space. The wide streets encourage cafes, pubs and restaurants to provide pavement seating. Lying between the offices at the [Golden Mile](/wiki/Golden_Mile_(Brentford) "Golden Mile (Brentford)") [Great West Road](/wiki/A4_road_(England) "A4 road (England)") and [Hammersmith](/wiki/Hammersmith "Hammersmith"), office developments and warehouse conversions to offices began from the 1960s. The first in 1961 was 414 Chiswick High Road on the site of the old [Chiswick Empire](/wiki/Chiswick_Empire "Chiswick Empire"). Between 1964 and 1966, the 18-storey [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") headquarters was built above [Gunnersbury station](/wiki/Gunnersbury_station "Gunnersbury station"), designed to accommodate 1500 people. It became the home of the [British Standards Institution](/wiki/BSI_Group "BSI Group") in 1994.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg1995137-49) Chiswick has an annual book festival.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Chiswick is home to the [Griffin Brewery](/wiki/Fuller%27s_Brewery "Fuller's Brewery"), where [Fuller, Smith & Turner](/wiki/Fuller,_Smith_%26_Turner "Fuller, Smith & Turner") and its predecessor companies brewed their prize-winning [ales](/wiki/Cask_ale "Cask ale") on the same site for over 350 years. The original brewery was in the gardens of Bedford House in Chiswick Mall.[[50]](#cite_note-History-51)

A weekly [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") is held every Sunday by Grove Park Farm House, Duke's Meadows.[[51]](#cite_note-52) A monthly flower market is held on the first Sunday of each month on Chiswick High Road in the old market place, now mostly used as a car park, near the Hogarth statue.[[52]](#cite_note-53) An antiques market is to be held on the second Sunday of each month, and a "Cheese and Provisions" market with 23 stalls on the third and fourth Sundays of each month in the same area, so there will in effect be a weekly market event on the High Road once again.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)

## Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Chiswick_House_from_SE_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Chiswick_House_from_SE_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chiswick_House_from_SE_(cropped).jpg)

[Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House") in [Palladian style](/wiki/Palladian_style "Palladian style"), 1726–29

### Chiswick House

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chiswick House")]

[Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House") was designed by the [Third Earl of Burlington](/wiki/Richard_Boyle,_3rd_Earl_of_Burlington "Richard Boyle, 3rd Earl of Burlington"), and built for him, in 1726–29 as an extension to an earlier [Jacobean](/wiki/Jacobean_architecture "Jacobean architecture") house (subsequently demolished in 1788); it is considered to be among the finest surviving examples of [Palladian architecture](/wiki/Palladian_architecture "Palladian architecture") in Britain, with superb collections of paintings and furniture. Its surrounding grounds, laid out by [William Kent](/wiki/William_Kent "William Kent"), are among the most important historical gardens in England and Wales, forming one of the first [English landscape gardens](/wiki/English_landscape_garden "English landscape garden").[[55]](#cite_note-56) It was used as [an asylum](/wiki/Chiswick_Asylum "Chiswick Asylum") from 1892 to 1928; up to 40 private patients were housed in wings which were demolished in 1956 when the house was restored.[[56]](#cite_note-Bethlem-57)

### Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Churches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Turnham_Green_Church_3.jpg/250px-Turnham_Green_Church_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turnham_Green_Church_3.jpg)

Christ Church, [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green "Turnham Green"), by [George Gilbert Scott](/wiki/George_Gilbert_Scott "George Gilbert Scott"), 1843

[St Nicholas Church](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Chiswick "St Nicholas Church, Chiswick"), near the river Thames, has a 15th-century tower, although the remainder of the church was rebuilt by [J.L. Pearson](/wiki/J.L._Pearson "J.L. Pearson") in 1882–84. Monuments in the churchyard mark the burial sites of the 18th-century English artist [William Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth") and [William Kent](/wiki/William_Kent "William Kent"), the architect and landscape designer; the churchyard also houses a mausoleum (for [Philip James de Loutherbourg](/wiki/Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg "Philip James de Loutherbourg")) designed by [John Soane](/wiki/John_Soane "John Soane"), and the tomb of [Josiah Wedgwood](/wiki/Josiah_Wedgwood "Josiah Wedgwood")'s business partner, [Thomas Bentley](/wiki/Thomas_Bentley_(manufacturer) "Thomas Bentley (manufacturer)"), designed by [Thomas Scheemakers](/wiki/Thomas_Scheemakers "Thomas Scheemakers").[[57]](#cite_note-58) One of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell")'s daughters, Mary Fauconberg, lived at Sutton Court and is buried in the churchyard.[[58]](#cite_note-Clegg30-59) Enduring legend has it that the body of Oliver Cromwell was also interred with her, though as the [Fauconbergs](/wiki/Viscount_Fauconberg "Viscount Fauconberg") did not move to Sutton Court until 15 years after his disinterment, it is more likely he was reburied at their home at [Newburgh Priory](/wiki/Newburgh_Priory "Newburgh Priory").[[58]](#cite_note-Clegg30-59) Private [Frederick Hitch](/wiki/Frederick_Hitch "Frederick Hitch") [VC](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross"), hero of [Rorke's Drift](/wiki/Rorke%27s_Drift "Rorke's Drift"), is also buried there.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

The church of [St Michael, Sutton Court](/wiki/St_Michael%27s_Church,_Grove_Park "St Michael's Church, Grove Park") was designed by [W. D. Caröe](/wiki/W._D._Car%C3%B6e "W. D. Caröe") in 1908–1909. It is a red brick building on Elmwood road, in Tudor style.[[60]](#cite_note-BHOchurches-61) St Paul's Church, Grove Park is a Gothic style stone building designed by H. Currey. It was built largely at the Duke of Devonshire's expense in 1872.[[60]](#cite_note-BHOchurches-61)

[St Michael and All Angels, Bedford Park](/wiki/St_Michael_and_All_Angels,_Bedford_Park "St Michael and All Angels, Bedford Park") was initially a temporary iron building from 1876 on Chiswick High Road facing Chiswick Lane. The current building's foundation stone was laid in 1879 and consecrated in 1880. It was designed, along with much of Bedford Park, by [Norman Shaw](/wiki/Norman_Shaw "Norman Shaw"), and was called "a very lovely church" by [John Betjeman](/wiki/John_Betjeman "John Betjeman"). It is an [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") church, and was attacked on the day it was consecrated for "Popish and Pagan mummeries" by the brewer Henry Smith, churchwarden of St Nicholas, Chiswick.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

[Christ Church, Turnham Green](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Turnham_Green "Christ Church, Turnham Green") is an early Victorian Gothic building of flint with stone dressings. The main part of the building, by [George Gilbert Scott](/wiki/George_Gilbert_Scott "George Gilbert Scott") and W. B. Moffat, is from 1843; the chancel and northeast chapel were added in 1887 by J. Brooks.[[60]](#cite_note-BHOchurches-61)

Chiswick's principal [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") church, [Our Lady of Grace and St Edward](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Grace_and_St_Edward_Church "Our Lady of Grace and St Edward Church") ([the Confessor](/wiki/St_Edward_the_Confessor "St Edward the Confessor")) in the [Diocese of Westminster](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Westminster "Roman Catholic Diocese of Westminster"), lies on the corner of Duke's Avenue and the High Road. It is a red brick building; the parish was founded in 1848, a school began c. 1855, and a church was opened by [Cardinal Wiseman](/wiki/Cardinal_Wiseman "Cardinal Wiseman") on the present site in 1864. It was replaced by the present building in 1886, opened by [Cardinal Manning](/wiki/Henry_Edward_Manning "Henry Edward Manning"). The heavy debts incurred were paid off and the church consecrated in 1904. The square tower was added after the First World War by Canon Egan as a war memorial.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

The [Cathedral of the Nativity of the Most Holy Mother of God and the Holy Royal Martyrs](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Nativity_of_the_Most_Holy_Mother_of_God_and_the_Holy_Royal_Martyrs "Cathedral of the Nativity of the Most Holy Mother of God and the Holy Royal Martyrs") with its characteristic blue onion dome with gold stars is in Harvard Road. The [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Diocese_of_Great_Britain_and_Western_Europe "Russian Orthodox Diocese of Great Britain and Western Europe") church built it in 1998.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Chiswick Mall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chiswick Mall")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/20210602_123430_Looking_east_along_Chiswick_Mall_from_Church_Street.jpg/500px-20210602_123430_Looking_east_along_Chiswick_Mall_from_Church_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:20210602_123430_Looking_east_along_Chiswick_Mall_from_Church_Street.jpg)

[Chiswick Mall](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall "Chiswick Mall"), looking east from Church Street. The grand houses are on the left; their waterfront gardens are on the right.

Main article: [Chiswick Mall](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall "Chiswick Mall")

[Chiswick Mall](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall "Chiswick Mall") is a waterfront street on the north bank of the River Thames in the oldest part of Chiswick near St Nicholas Church. It consists mainly of some thirty "grand houses"[[64]](#cite_note-Clegg_2021-65) from the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") and [Victorian eras](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), many of them now listed buildings, overlooking the street on the north side; their gardens are on the other side of the street beside the river.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The largest and finest[[64]](#cite_note-Clegg_2021-65) house on the street is [Walpole House](/wiki/Walpole_House "Walpole House"), a Grade I listed building; part of it is Tudor, but the building now visible is late 17th to early 18th century.[[66]](#cite_note-HE_Walpole-67)

### Strand-on-the-Green

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Strand-on-the-Green")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Kew_Bridge_and_Strand-on-the-Green%2C_1832.jpg/250px-Kew_Bridge_and_Strand-on-the-Green%2C_1832.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kew_Bridge_and_Strand-on-the-Green,_1832.jpg)

Engraving of [Kew Bridge](/wiki/Kew_Bridge "Kew Bridge") and [Strand-on-the-Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green"), 1832

Main article: [Strand-on-the-Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green")

Strand-on-the-Green is the most westerly part of Chiswick, "particularly picturesque"[[67]](#cite_note-SOTG_LBH_2018-68) with a paved riverside path fronted by a row of "imposing"[[67]](#cite_note-SOTG_LBH_2018-68) 18th-century houses, interspersed with three riverside pubs, the Bell and Crown, Bull’s Head, and the City Barge. The low-lying path is flooded at high tides. It became fashionable in 1759 when [Kew Bridge](/wiki/Kew_Bridge "Kew Bridge") opened just upstream, with the royal family at [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace") nearby.[[67]](#cite_note-SOTG_LBH_2018-68)

### Bedford Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bedford Park")]

Main article: [Bedford Park, London](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London")

The [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London") neighbourhood was described by [Nikolaus Pevsner](/wiki/Nikolaus_Pevsner "Nikolaus Pevsner") as the first place "where the relaxed, informal mood of a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") or village was adopted for a complete speculatively built suburb".[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECherryPevsner1991406–410-69) In 1877 the speculator [Jonathan Carr](/wiki/Jonathan_Carr_(property_developer) "Jonathan Carr (property developer)") hired Shaw as his estate architect. Shaw's house designs, in the [Queen Anne Revival style](/wiki/British_Queen_Anne_Revival_architecture "British Queen Anne Revival architecture") with red brick, [roughcast](/wiki/Roughcast "Roughcast"), decorative [gables](/wiki/Gable "Gable"), and [both oriel](/wiki/Oriel_window "Oriel window") and [dormer windows](/wiki/Dormer_window "Dormer window"), gave the impression of great variety using only a few types of house.[[69]](#cite_note-Clegg_People-70) These were scaled-down versions of the more expensive houses that he had designed for wealthy areas such as [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea,_London "Chelsea, London"), [Hampstead](/wiki/Hampstead "Hampstead"), and [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington "Kensington"). He also designed the focal buildings of the garden suburb, including the church of St Michael and All Angels and the Tabard Inn opposite it.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECherryPevsner1991406–410-69)[[70]](#cite_note-Girouard_1984-71)[[71]](#cite_note-Hidden-72)

### Duke's Meadows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Duke's Meadows")]

Main article: [Duke's Meadows](/wiki/Duke%27s_Meadows "Duke's Meadows")

[Duke's Meadows](/wiki/Duke%27s_Meadows "Duke's Meadows") stands on land formerly owned by the [Duke of Devonshire](/wiki/Duke_of_Devonshire "Duke of Devonshire"). In the 1920s, it was purchased by the local council, who developed it as a recreational centre. A promenade and bandstand were built, and the meadows are still used for sport with a rugby club, football pitches, hockey club, several rowing clubs and a golf club. In recent years a local conservation charity, the Dukes Meadows Trust, has undertaken extensive restoration work, which saw a long-term project of a children's water play area opened in August 2006.[[72]](#cite_note-dukes-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Pond_Dippers_on_Frog_Day_in_Gunnersbury_Triangle.JPG/250px-Pond_Dippers_on_Frog_Day_in_Gunnersbury_Triangle.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pond_Dippers_on_Frog_Day_in_Gunnersbury_Triangle.JPG)

Pond dipping in [Gunnersbury Triangle](/wiki/Gunnersbury_Triangle "Gunnersbury Triangle"), a local nature reserve

### Gunnersbury Triangle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Gunnersbury Triangle")]

Main article: [Gunnersbury Triangle](/wiki/Gunnersbury_Triangle "Gunnersbury Triangle")

The [Gunnersbury Triangle](/wiki/Gunnersbury_Triangle "Gunnersbury Triangle") [local nature reserve](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve"), opposite Chiswick Park Underground station, is managed by [London Wildlife Trust](/wiki/London_Wildlife_Trust "London Wildlife Trust"). The area, a railway triangle, was saved from development by a public inquiry, and became a reserve in 1985. Its 2.5 hectares are covered mainly in secondary [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch") woodland, with [willow](/wiki/Willow "Willow") [carr](/wiki/Carr_(landform) "Carr (landform)") (wet woodland) in the low-lying centre, and [acid grassland](/wiki/Acid_grassland "Acid grassland") on the former Acton Curve railway track. The reserve runs a varied programme of activities including wildlife walks, fungus forays, open days and talks.[[73]](#cite_note-LNR-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-LWT-GT-76)

### Public houses and theatres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public houses and theatres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Mawson_Arms_01.JPG/250px-Mawson_Arms_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mawson_Arms_01.JPG)

The [Mawson Arms](/wiki/Mawson_Arms "Mawson Arms"), briefly the home of the poet [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope")

There are several historic [public houses](/wiki/Public_house "Public house") in Chiswick, some of them [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"), including the [Mawson Arms](/wiki/Mawson_Arms "Mawson Arms"),[[76]](#cite_note-77) the [George and Devonshire](/wiki/George_and_Devonshire "George and Devonshire"),[[77]](#cite_note-78) the [Old Packhorse](/wiki/Old_Packhorse "Old Packhorse")[[78]](#cite_note-79) and [The Tabard](/wiki/The_Tabard_(Chiswick) "The Tabard (Chiswick)") in Bath Road near Turnham Green station. The Tabard is known for its [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris") interior and its Norman Shaw exterior; it was built in 1880.[[79]](#cite_note-English_Heritage-80) Three more pubs are in [Strand-on-the-Green](/wiki/Strand-on-the-Green "Strand-on-the-Green"), fronting on to the Thames river path.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

Chiswick had two well-known theatres in the 20th century.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClegg199595–97-82) The [Chiswick Empire](/wiki/Chiswick_Empire "Chiswick Empire") (1912 to 1959) was at 414 Chiswick High Road. It had 2,140 seats,[[82]](#cite_note-83) and staged [music hall](/wiki/Music_hall "Music hall") entertainment, plays, reviews, opera, ballet and an annual Christmas [pantomime](/wiki/Pantomime "Pantomime"). The Q Theatre (1924 to 1959) was a small theatre opposite Kew Bridge station. It staged the first works of [Terence Rattigan](/wiki/Terence_Rattigan "Terence Rattigan") and [William Douglas-Home](/wiki/William_Douglas-Home "William Douglas-Home"), and many of its plays went on to the West End.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

The 96-seat [Tabard Theatre](/wiki/Tabard_Theatre "Tabard Theatre") (1985) in Bath Road, upstairs from the Tabard pub but a separate business, is known for new writing and experimental work.[[84]](#cite_note-Tabard-85)

### Other buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other buildings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Voysey_House_Sandersons_Building_design_by_CFA_Voysey_1902.jpg/250px-Voysey_House_Sandersons_Building_design_by_CFA_Voysey_1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Voysey_House_Sandersons_Building_design_by_CFA_Voysey_1902.jpg)

[Sanderson](/wiki/Arthur_Sanderson_%26_Sons "Arthur Sanderson & Sons") wallpaper factory design by [Charles Voysey](/wiki/Charles_Voysey_(architect) "Charles Voysey (architect)"), 1902

The [Sanderson](/wiki/Arthur_Sanderson_%26_Sons "Arthur Sanderson & Sons") Factory in Barley Mow Passage, now known as Voysey House, was designed by the architect [Charles Voysey](/wiki/Charles_Voysey_(architect) "Charles Voysey (architect)") in 1902. It is built in white glazed brick, with [Staffordshire blue bricks](/wiki/Staffordshire_blue_brick "Staffordshire blue brick") (now painted black) forming horizontal bands, the plinth, and surrounds for door and window openings, and dressings in [Portland stone](/wiki/Portland_stone "Portland stone"). It was originally a wallpaper printing works, now used as office space. It is a Grade II\* [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"). It faces the main factory building and was once joined to it by a bridge across the road. It was Voysey's only industrial building, and is considered an "important [Arts and Crafts](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts "Arts and Crafts") factory building".[[85]](#cite_note-86)

In 1971, [Erin Pizzey](/wiki/Erin_Pizzey "Erin Pizzey") established the world's first domestic violence refuge at 2 Belmont Terrace, naming her organisation "[Chiswick Women's Aid](/wiki/Chiswick_Women%27s_Aid "Chiswick Women's Aid")". The local council attempted to evict Pizzey's residents, but were unsuccessful and she soon established more such premises elsewhere, inspiring the creation of refuges worldwide.[[86]](#cite_note-87)[[87]](#cite_note-88)

Chiswick is home to the [Arts Educational Schools](/wiki/Arts_Educational_Schools "Arts Educational Schools") in Bath Road.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

The house used for filming the comedy show *[Taskmaster](/wiki/Taskmaster_(TV_series) "Taskmaster (TV series)")*, a former groundskeeper's cottage, is just off Great Chertsey Road, near [Chiswick Bridge](/wiki/Chiswick_Bridge "Chiswick Bridge").[[89]](#cite_note-90)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Chiswick-Bridge-15-540-3.jpg/500px-Chiswick-Bridge-15-540-3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chiswick-Bridge-15-540-3.jpg)

The design of [Chiswick Bridge](/wiki/Chiswick_Bridge "Chiswick Bridge"), opened in 1933, has been praised as reflecting the [Palladian architecture](/wiki/Palladian_architecture "Palladian architecture") of [Chiswick House](/wiki/Chiswick_House "Chiswick House").[[90]](#cite_note-91)

Chiswick is situated at the start of the [North Circular Road](/wiki/A406_road "A406 road") (A406), [South Circular Road](/wiki/A205_road "A205 road") (A205) and the [M4 motorway](/wiki/M4_motorway "M4 motorway"), the latter providing a direct connection to [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport") and the [M25 motorway](/wiki/M25_motorway "M25 motorway"). The [Great West Road](/wiki/A4_road_(Great_Britain) "A4 road (Great Britain)") (A4) runs eastwards into central London via the [Hogarth Roundabout](/wiki/Hogarth_Roundabout "Hogarth Roundabout") where it meets the [Great Chertsey Road](/wiki/A316_road "A316 road") (A316) which runs south-west, eventually joining the [M3 motorway](/wiki/M3_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M3 motorway (Great Britain)").[[91]](#cite_note-92)

The southern border of Chiswick runs along the River Thames, which is crossed in this area by [Barnes Railway and Foot Bridge](/wiki/Barnes_Railway_Bridge "Barnes Railway Bridge"), [Chiswick Bridge](/wiki/Chiswick_Bridge "Chiswick Bridge"), [Kew Railway Bridge](/wiki/Kew_Railway_Bridge "Kew Railway Bridge") and [Kew Bridge](/wiki/Kew_Bridge "Kew Bridge"). River services between [Westminster Pier](/wiki/Westminster_Pier "Westminster Pier") and [Hampton Court](/wiki/Hampton_Court "Hampton Court") depart from [Kew Gardens Pier](/wiki/Kew_Gardens_Pier "Kew Gardens Pier") just across Kew Bridge.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

Bus routes on or near Chiswick High Road are the [94](/wiki/London_Buses_route_94 "London Buses route 94"), [110](/wiki/London_Buses_route_110 "London Buses route 110"), [237](/wiki/London_Buses_route_237 "London Buses route 237"), [267](/wiki/London_Buses_route_267 "London Buses route 267"), [272](/wiki/London_Buses_route_272 "London Buses route 272"), [440](/wiki/London_Buses_route_440 "London Buses route 440"), [E3](/wiki/London_Buses_route_E3 "London Buses route E3") and [H91](/wiki/London_Buses_route_H91 "London Buses route H91").[[93]](#cite_note-94) The 94 is a 24-hour service, and the High Road is also served at night by the [N9](/wiki/London_Buses_route_N9 "London Buses route N9").[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)

The [District line](/wiki/District_line "District line") serves Chiswick with four [London Underground stations](/wiki/List_of_London_Underground_stations "List of London Underground stations"), [Stamford Brook](/wiki/Stamford_Brook_tube_station "Stamford Brook tube station"), [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green_tube_station "Turnham Green tube station"), [Chiswick Park](/wiki/Chiswick_Park_tube_station "Chiswick Park tube station") and [Gunnersbury](/wiki/Gunnersbury_tube_station "Gunnersbury tube station").[[96]](#cite_note-LudlowThompson-97) Turnham Green is an interchange with the [Piccadilly line](/wiki/Piccadilly_line "Piccadilly line"), but only before 06:50 and after 22:30, when Piccadilly line trains stop at the station. [Chiswick railway station](/wiki/Chiswick_railway_station "Chiswick railway station") on the [Hounslow Loop Line](/wiki/Hounslow_Loop_Line "Hounslow Loop Line") is served by a regular [South Western Railway](/wiki/South_Western_Railway_(train_operating_company) "South Western Railway (train operating company)") service to [London Waterloo](/wiki/London_Waterloo_railway_station "London Waterloo railway station") via [Clapham Junction](/wiki/Clapham_Junction_railway_station "Clapham Junction railway station").[[96]](#cite_note-LudlowThompson-97) The [North London line](/wiki/North_London_line "North London line") crosses Chiswick (north-south); [London Overground](/wiki/London_Overground "London Overground") stations are [Gunnersbury](/wiki/Gunnersbury_railway_station "Gunnersbury railway station") and [South Acton](/wiki/South_Acton_railway_station_(England) "South Acton railway station (England)").[[96]](#cite_note-LudlowThompson-97)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Boat_Race_Finish_post.jpg/250px-Boat_Race_Finish_post.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boat_Race_Finish_post.jpg)

[The Boat Race](/wiki/The_Boat_Race "The Boat Race") finishing post by Chiswick Bridge

Chiswick's local rugby union teams include Chiswick RFC, formerly Old Meadonians RFC. The team plays league games on a Saturday at Dukes Meadows.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Chiswick's cricket club, formerly known as Turnham Green and Polytechnic, plays at Riverside Drive.[[98]](#cite_note-99) On Chiswick Common is the Rocks Lane Multi Sports Centre, where there are tennis, five-a-side football and netball courts available to hire to the public.[[99]](#cite_note-100) Private tennis coaching for individuals and groups is also available.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

The Chiswick reach of the Thames is heavily used for competitive and recreational [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"). [Championship Course](/wiki/The_Championship_Course "The Championship Course") from [Mortlake](/wiki/Mortlake "Mortlake") to [Putney](/wiki/Putney "Putney") runs past Chiswick Eyot and Duke's Meadows. [The Boat Race](/wiki/The_Boat_Race "The Boat Race") is contested on the Championship Course on a flood tide (in other words from Putney to Mortlake) with Duke's Meadows a popular view-point for the closing stages of the race. The finishing post is just downstream of Chiswick Bridge.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Other important races such as the [Head of the River Race](/wiki/Head_of_the_River_Race "Head of the River Race") race the reverse course, on an ebb tide.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Chiswick is home to several clubs. The [University of London Boat Club](/wiki/University_of_London_Boat_Club "University of London Boat Club") is based in its boathouse off Hartington Road, which also houses the clubs of many London colleges and teaching hospitals; recent members include [Tim Foster](/wiki/Tim_Foster "Tim Foster"), Gold medallist at the Sydney Olympics and [Frances Houghton](/wiki/Frances_Houghton "Frances Houghton"), World Champion in 2005, 2006 and 2007.[[103]](#cite_note-104) [Quintin Boat Club](/wiki/Quintin_Boat_Club "Quintin Boat Club") lies between Chiswick Quay Marina and Chiswick Bridge.[[104]](#cite_note-105) [Tideway Scullers School](/wiki/Tideway_Scullers_School "Tideway Scullers School") is just downriver of Chiswick Bridge; its members include single sculling World Champion [Mahé Drysdale](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9_Drysdale "Mahé Drysdale") and Great Britain single sculler [Alan Campbell](/wiki/Alan_Campbell_(sculler) "Alan Campbell (sculler)").[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Chiswick High Road was once home to the *Chequered Flag* garage and its associated [motor racing](/wiki/Motor_racing "Motor racing") team.[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notable people")]

Further information: [St Nicholas Church, Chiswick](/wiki/St_Nicholas_Church,_Chiswick "St Nicholas Church, Chiswick")

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 17th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Mary_Cromwell%2C_Countess_Fauconberg%2C_by_circle_of_Thomas_Murray%2C_c_1700.jpg/250px-Mary_Cromwell%2C_Countess_Fauconberg%2C_by_circle_of_Thomas_Murray%2C_c_1700.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary_Cromwell,_Countess_Fauconberg,_by_circle_of_Thomas_Murray,_c_1700.jpg)

[Mary Cromwell, Countess Fauconberg](/wiki/Mary_Cromwell,_Countess_Fauconberg "Mary Cromwell, Countess Fauconberg"), c. 1700

[Mary Cromwell](/wiki/Mary_Cromwell,_Countess_Fauconberg "Mary Cromwell, Countess Fauconberg"), daughter of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), lived in [Sutton Manor](/wiki/Sutton_Manor,_Chiswick "Sutton Manor, Chiswick") in Little Sutton from 1676 to her death in 1713. She was married to [Thomas Belasyse, 1st Earl Fauconberg](/wiki/Thomas_Belasyse,_1st_Earl_Fauconberg "Thomas Belasyse, 1st Earl Fauconberg"), who supported Parliament during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 18th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Hogarths_house_5832.JPG/250px-Hogarths_house_5832.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hogarths_house_5832.JPG)

[Hogarth's House](/wiki/Hogarth%27s_House "Hogarth's House"), later the home of the poet [Henry Francis Cary](/wiki/Henry_Francis_Cary "Henry Francis Cary")

In the 18th century, the poet [Alexander Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope"), author of *[The Rape of the Lock](/wiki/The_Rape_of_the_Lock "The Rape of the Lock")*, lived in Chiswick between 1716 and 1719, in the building which is now the [Mawson Arms](/wiki/Mawson_Arms "Mawson Arms") at the corner of Mawson Lane.[[110]](#cite_note-111)[[111]](#cite_note-112) The actor [Charles Holland](/wiki/Charles_Holland_(actor) "Charles Holland (actor)") was born in Chiswick in 1733.[[112]](#cite_note-113) The artist [William Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth") bought the house now known as [Hogarth's House](/wiki/Hogarth%27s_House "Hogarth's House") in 1749,[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115) lived there until his death in 1764, and is buried in St Nicholas's churchyard.[[115]](#cite_note-116)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECherryPevsner1991404-117) The house later belonged to the poet and translator of [Dante](/wiki/Dante "Dante"), [Henry Francis Cary](/wiki/Henry_Francis_Cary "Henry Francis Cary"), who lived there from 1814 to 1833.[[117]](#cite_note-118) In February 1766 [Jean-Jacques Rousseau](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Rousseau "Jean-Jacques Rousseau") lived a few weeks with a local grocer, before moving to [Wootton, Staffordshire](/wiki/Wootton,_Staffordshire "Wootton, Staffordshire").[[118]](#cite_note-119) The painter [Johann Zoffany](/wiki/Johann_Zoffany "Johann Zoffany") lived on Strand-on-the-Green.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Bath_Road%2C_London_by_Camille_Pissarro.jpg/250px-Bath_Road%2C_London_by_Camille_Pissarro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bath_Road,_London_by_Camille_Pissarro.jpg)

*[Bath Road](/wiki/Bath_Road,_London "Bath Road, London"), London*, 1897. [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionist "Impressionist") painting of Bath Road, [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_London "Bedford Park, London") by [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro")

Two prominent early 19th century politicians both died, in the same room, at Chiswick House - [Whig](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_Kingdom) "Whig Party (United Kingdom)") [Foreign Secretary](/wiki/Foreign_Secretary_(United_Kingdom) "Foreign Secretary (United Kingdom)") [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox") in 1806[[120]](#cite_note-121) and [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") [Prime Minister](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of Prime Ministers of the United Kingdom") [George Canning](/wiki/George_Canning "George Canning") in 1827.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

The Italian writer, revolutionary and poet [Ugo Foscolo](/wiki/Ugo_Foscolo "Ugo Foscolo") died in exile at Turnham Green in 1827,[[122]](#cite_note-123) and was buried at St Nicholas Churchyard, Chiswick, where his monument remains, although in 1871 his body was taken to Italy and given a national hero's burial in [Santa Croce, Florence](/wiki/Santa_Croce,_Florence "Santa Croce, Florence") alongside [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo") and [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo"), while his monument in Chiswick was lavishly refurbished.[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125)

The inventor of the [electric telegraph](/wiki/Electrical_telegraph "Electrical telegraph"), [Francis Ronalds](/wiki/Francis_Ronalds "Francis Ronalds"), lived on Chiswick Lane from 1833 to 1852.[[125]](#cite_note-126) Another engineer, [John Edward Thornycroft](/wiki/John_Edward_Thornycroft "John Edward Thornycroft") was born in Chiswick in 1872;[[126]](#cite_note-127) his father, [John Isaac Thornycroft](/wiki/John_Isaac_Thornycroft "John Isaac Thornycroft"), had founded the Chiswick-based [John I. Thornycroft & Company](/wiki/John_I._Thornycroft_%26_Company "John I. Thornycroft & Company") shipbuilding company in 1864, which Thornycroft later joined and developed.[[127]](#cite_note-128) The artist [Montague Dawson](/wiki/Montague_Dawson "Montague Dawson"), regarded as one of the best 20th-century [painters of the sea](/wiki/Marine_art "Marine art"), was born in Chiswick in 1895.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Sketch_of_Petersham_Methodist_Chapel_and_Turnham_Green_Congregational_Church_by_Vincent_Van_Gogh_c1875.jpg/330px-Sketch_of_Petersham_Methodist_Chapel_and_Turnham_Green_Congregational_Church_by_Vincent_Van_Gogh_c1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sketch_of_Petersham_Methodist_Chapel_and_Turnham_Green_Congregational_Church_by_Vincent_Van_Gogh_c1875.jpg)

Sketch of Turnham Green Congregational Church (right) by [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh"), c. 1875. He taught Sunday school in the iron structure.[[129]](#cite_note-Clegg_2001_van_Gogh-130)

The painter [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh") spent three years in Chiswick in the 1870s, teaching Sunday school pupils in the newly-constructed Chiswick Congregational Church, which was on the site of the Arlington Park Mansions on Turnham Green; he wrote of Chiswick as a "verdant" district of London.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132)[[129]](#cite_note-Clegg_2001_van_Gogh-130)

The poet [W. B. Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats") lived in Woodstock Road as a boy from 1879, and came back in 1887 to live in Blenheim Road, where, inspired by Chiswick Eyot, he wrote *[The Lake Isle of Innisfree](/wiki/The_Lake_Isle_of_Innisfree "The Lake Isle of Innisfree")*.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)

The Pissarro family of painters, the impressionist [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"), his eldest son [Lucien](/wiki/Lucien_Pissarro "Lucien Pissarro"), as well as Felix and Ludovic-Rodo lived in 62 Bath Road, Chiswick around 1897; with Camille Pissarro painting a series of notable landscapes of the area.[[134]](#cite_note-135)[[135]](#cite_note-136) The landscape artist [Lewis Pinhorn Wood](/wiki/Lewis_Pinhorn_Wood "Lewis Pinhorn Wood") lived at Homefield Road[[136]](#cite_note-137) from 1897 to 1908.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Arlington_Park_Mansions_-_Entrance.jpg/250px-Arlington_Park_Mansions_-_Entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arlington_Park_Mansions_-_Entrance.jpg)

Arlington Park Mansions, facing [Turnham Green](/wiki/Turnham_Green "Turnham Green"), with [E. M. Forster](/wiki/E._M._Forster "E. M. Forster") blue plaque

In the twentieth century, the novelist [E. M. Forster](/wiki/E._M._Forster "E. M. Forster") (1879–1970) lived at 9 Arlington Park Mansions[[137]](#cite_note-138) from 1939 until at least 1961.[[138]](#cite_note-139) [John Osborne](/wiki/John_Osborne "John Osborne") (1929–1994) wrote his play *[Look Back in Anger](/wiki/Look_Back_in_Anger "Look Back in Anger")* on his [houseboat](/wiki/Houseboat "Houseboat") at Cubitts Yacht Basin.[[139]](#cite_note-CBF_Writers_Trail-140)

Notable people born before the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") include the cricketers [Patsy Hendren](/wiki/Patsy_Hendren "Patsy Hendren") (1899–1962)[[140]](#cite_note-141) and [Jack Robertson](/wiki/Jack_Robertson_(English_cricketer) "Jack Robertson (English cricketer)") (1917–1996),[[141]](#cite_note-142) the editor and publisher [Kaye Webb](/wiki/Kaye_Webb "Kaye Webb") (1914-1996),[[142]](#cite_note-143) the novelist [Iris Murdoch](/wiki/Iris_Murdoch "Iris Murdoch") (1919–1999) who lived on Eastbourne Road,[[143]](#cite_note-W4_Murdoch-144) the theatre and film director [Peter Brook](/wiki/Peter_Brook "Peter Brook") (1925–2022),[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-Kustow2013-146) the [Winchester College](/wiki/Winchester_College "Winchester College") headmaster [John Leonard Thorn](/wiki/John_Thorn_(headmaster) "John Thorn (headmaster)") (1925–2023),[[146]](#cite_note-Telegraph_Obituary-147) the zoologist and broadcaster [Aubrey Manning](/wiki/Aubrey_Manning "Aubrey Manning") (1930–2018),[[147]](#cite_note-148) and marine geologist [Frederick Vine](/wiki/Frederick_Vine "Frederick Vine") (1939– ).[[148]](#cite_note-149) The comic song performer [Michael Flanders](/wiki/Michael_Flanders "Michael Flanders") (1922–1975) spent the last years of his life in Bedford Park.[[149]](#cite_note-150) The actress [Sylvia Syms](/wiki/Sylvia_Syms "Sylvia Syms") (1934–2023), star of films such as *[Ice Cold in Alex](/wiki/Ice_Cold_in_Alex "Ice Cold in Alex")*, lived on Dukes Avenue.[[150]](#cite_note-151)
[The Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who") rock musicians [John Entwistle](/wiki/John_Entwistle "John Entwistle") (1944–2002) and [Pete Townshend](/wiki/Pete_Townshend "Pete Townshend") (1945– ) were both born in Chiswick during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").[[151]](#cite_note-152) [Deep Purple](/wiki/Deep_Purple "Deep Purple") lead singer [Ian Gillan](/wiki/Ian_Gillan "Ian Gillan") was born in Chiswick on 19 August 1945.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Those born in Chiswick during the post-war period include the rock musician [Dave Cousins](/wiki/Dave_Cousins "Dave Cousins"),[[153]](#cite_note-Cousins_and_Donegan-154) the cricketer [Mike Selvey](/wiki/Mike_Selvey "Mike Selvey") (1948– ),[[154]](#cite_note-155) the musician [Phil Collins](/wiki/Phil_Collins "Phil Collins") (1951– ),[[155]](#cite_note-156) the singer [Kim Wilde](/wiki/Kim_Wilde "Kim Wilde") (1960– ),[[156]](#cite_note-157) illustrator [Clifford Harper](/wiki/Clifford_Harper "Clifford Harper") (1949– ), the photographer [Derek Ridgers](/wiki/Derek_Ridgers "Derek Ridgers") (1950– ),[[157]](#cite_note-158) the actress [Kate Beckinsale](/wiki/Kate_Beckinsale "Kate Beckinsale") (1973– ),[[158]](#cite_note-159) the comedian [Mel Smith](/wiki/Mel_Smith "Mel Smith") (1952–2013),[[159]](#cite_note-160) and the cricketer [Dimitri Mascarenhas](/wiki/Dimitri_Mascarenhas "Dimitri Mascarenhas") (1977– ).[[160]](#cite_note-161)

Among those who have lived in Chiswick are the novelist [Anthony Burgess](/wiki/Anthony_Burgess "Anthony Burgess") (1917–1993), at 24 Glebe Street in the mid-1960s;[[161]](#cite_note-162) the playwright [Harold Pinter](/wiki/Harold_Pinter "Harold Pinter") (1930–2008) who lived at 373 Chiswick High Road;[[143]](#cite_note-W4_Murdoch-144) the pianist and broadcaster [Sidney Harrison](/wiki/Sidney_Harrison "Sidney Harrison") (1903–1986) who in the 1960s lived at 57 Hartington Road[[162]](#cite_note-163) and later at 37 The Avenue;[[163]](#cite_note-164) the musical double act [Bob and Alf Pearson](/wiki/Bob_and_Alf_Pearson "Bob and Alf Pearson"), Bob (1907–1985) on Netheravon Road in the 1940s,[[164]](#cite_note-165) and Alf (1910–2012) on Linden Gardens in the 1950s;[[165]](#cite_note-166) the pop artist [Peter Blake](/wiki/Peter_Blake_(artist) "Peter Blake (artist)") (1932–), in Chiswick since 1967,[[166]](#cite_note-167) with a "vast" studio in a former [ironmonger](/wiki/Ironmonger "Ironmonger")'s warehouse;[[167]](#cite_note-Guardian-168) the actor [Hugh Grant](/wiki/Hugh_Grant "Hugh Grant") (1960– ), who grew up in Chiswick, living next to Arlington Park Mansions on Sutton Lane; the singer [Bruce Dickinson](/wiki/Bruce_Dickinson "Bruce Dickinson") (1958– ) of the band [Iron Maiden](/wiki/Iron_Maiden "Iron Maiden");[[168]](#cite_note-169) the TV presenter [Kate Humble](/wiki/Kate_Humble "Kate Humble") (1968– );[[169]](#cite_note-170) the actress [Elizabeth McGovern](/wiki/Elizabeth_McGovern "Elizabeth McGovern") (1961– ) and her husband, film director [Simon Curtis](/wiki/Simon_Curtis_(filmmaker) "Simon Curtis (filmmaker)") (1960– );[[170]](#cite_note-171) the American lawyer [John Lowenthal](/wiki/John_Lowenthal "John Lowenthal") (1925–2003),[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173) the actor [Imogen Stubbs](/wiki/Imogen_Stubbs "Imogen Stubbs") who lived on a barge[[173]](#cite_note-174) and attended Cavendish Primary School,[[174]](#cite_note-weale2018-175) the singer [Lonnie Donegan](/wiki/Lonnie_Donegan "Lonnie Donegan"),[[153]](#cite_note-Cousins_and_Donegan-154) the musician and songwriter [Noel Gallagher](/wiki/Noel_Gallagher "Noel Gallagher") (1967–),[[175]](#cite_note-176) the model [Cara Delevingne](/wiki/Cara_Delevingne "Cara Delevingne") (1992– ),[[176]](#cite_note-W424Nov2014-177) and the athlete [Georgia Hunter Bell](/wiki/Georgia_Hunter_Bell "Georgia Hunter Bell") (1993– ) who went to school in Chiswick.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The playwright [Michael Frayn](/wiki/Michael_Frayn "Michael Frayn") (1933– ) and his daughter the film maker and novelist [Rebecca Frayn](/wiki/Rebecca_Frayn "Rebecca Frayn") live in Chiswick.[[178]](#cite_note-179) Chiswick residents have included the singer [Sophie Ellis-Bextor](/wiki/Sophie_Ellis-Bextor "Sophie Ellis-Bextor"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) the TV journalists [Jeremy Vine](/wiki/Jeremy_Vine "Jeremy Vine"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) [Rageh Omaar](/wiki/Rageh_Omaar "Rageh Omaar")[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) and [Fergal Keane](/wiki/Fergal_Keane "Fergal Keane"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) the actors [Phyllis Logan](/wiki/Phyllis_Logan "Phyllis Logan"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) [Colin Firth](/wiki/Colin_Firth "Colin Firth"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) [David Tennant](/wiki/David_Tennant "David Tennant"), [Georgia Tennant](/wiki/Georgia_Tennant "Georgia Tennant"),[[180]](#cite_note-181) and [Vanessa Redgrave](/wiki/Vanessa_Redgrave "Vanessa Redgrave"),[[181]](#cite_note-182) the TV presenters [Clare Balding](/wiki/Clare_Balding "Clare Balding"),[[176]](#cite_note-W424Nov2014-177) [Sarah Greene](/wiki/Sarah_Greene "Sarah Greene"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) [Gavin Campbell](/wiki/Gavin_Campbell_(presenter) "Gavin Campbell (presenter)"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) [Mary Nightingale](/wiki/Mary_Nightingale "Mary Nightingale")[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) and [Tim Lovejoy](/wiki/Tim_Lovejoy "Tim Lovejoy"),[[182]](#cite_note-183) the journalist [Alice Arnold](/wiki/Alice_Arnold_(broadcaster) "Alice Arnold (broadcaster)"),[[179]](#cite_note-W4_2016-180) and the celebrity duo [Anthony McPartlin and Declan Donnelly](/wiki/Ant_%26_Dec "Ant & Dec").[[183]](#cite_note-184)

## In the arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiswick&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: In the arts")]

Further information: [Chiswick Mall § In culture](/wiki/Chiswick_Mall#In_culture "Chiswick Mall")

The 1847/8 novel *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(novel) "Vanity Fair (novel)")* by [William Makepeace Thackeray](/wiki/William_Makepeace_Thackeray "William Makepeace Thackeray") opens at Miss Pinkerton's Academy for Young Ladies in Chiswick Mall. [Louis N. Parker](/wiki/Louis_N._Parker "Louis N. Parker")'s 1910 play *[Pomander Walk](/wiki/Pomander_Walk_(play) "Pomander Walk (play)")*has the imagined setting of "a retired crescent of five very small, old-fashioned houses near Chiswick, on the river-bank. ... They are exactly alike: miniature copies of Queen Anne mansions".[[185]](#cite_note-186) [Ford Madox Ford](/wiki/Ford_Madox_Ford "Ford Madox Ford")'s 1924/28 *[Parade's End](/wiki/Parade%27s_End "Parade's End")* tetralogy contains many scenes set in Chiswick, where the Wannop family resides. The BBC adaptation of the literary work featured filming on Bedford Park's Woodstock Road.[[186]](#cite_note-187) [Basil Dearden](/wiki/Basil_Dearden "Basil Dearden")'s 1961 [suspense film](/wiki/Suspense_film "Suspense film") *[Victim](/wiki/Victim_(1961_film) "Victim (1961 film)")*, starring [Dirk Bogarde](/wiki/Dirk_Bogarde "Dirk Bogarde") as the barrister Melville Farr, was set in Chiswick, and many of its scenes were filmed on Chiswick Mall, where Farr lived.[[187]](#cite_note-188) On 20 May 1966 the Beatles filmed two of their earliest promotional films for the songs "[Paperback Writer](/wiki/Paperback_Writer "Paperback Writer")" and "Rain" in the grounds of Chiswick House.[[188]](#cite_note-189) The 1991 official video of the [Paul Young](/wiki/Paul_Young "Paul Young") and [Zucchero](/wiki/Zucchero_Fornaciari "Zucchero Fornaciari") duet, *[Senza una donna](/wiki/Senza_una_donna "Senza una donna")* was filmed in a church hall in Chiswick.[[189]](#cite_note-190) The BBC sitcom *[My Family](/wiki/My_Family "My Family")* was set in Chiswick; it ran from 2000 to 2011.[[190]](#cite_note-W4_My_Family-191)
